title: Frequently confused words: assorted
description: Can you identify when to correctly use these words that often get confused with other words?

assessment_items.question_content:
**Identify whether to use _accept_ or _except_ in this sentence.**

> Mrs. Gupta could not be here tonight, so I \_\_\_\_\_\_ this award on her behalf.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
People often confuse the words "accept" and "except":

* **accept** means "to receive", "to take in", or "to believe"

 *The scientist **accepted** the Nobel prize.*

* **except** means "not including" or "everything but"

 *I love vegetables, **except** for broccoli.*
This sentence needs a verb that means "to receive", so we should use **accept**.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Identify whether to use _accept_ or _except_ in this sentence.**

> Everyone was ready to leave for Space World amusement park \_\_\_\_\_\_ Eric, who was just waking up.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
People often confuse the words "accept" and "except":

* **accept** means "to receive", "to take in", or "to believe"

 *The scientist **accepted** the Nobel prize.*

* **except** means "not including" or "everything but"

 *I love vegetables, **except** for broccoli.*
This sentence needs a word that means "not including", so we should use **except**.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Identify whether to use _advice_ or _advise_ in this sentence.**

> I’m trying to buy a new lawn mower but don’t know where to start looking. What would you \_\_\_\_\_\_?  


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
People often confuse the words "advice" and "advise":

* **advice** is a noun that means wisdom, counsel, or help

 *My teacher gave me good **advice** on how to improve my essay.*

* **advise** is a verb that means "to give advice"

 *I **advise** against eating a raw ghost pepper.*
This sentence needs a verb, so we should use **advise**.

* **advise** is a verb that means "to give advice"

This sentence needs a noun, so we should use **advice**.

assessment_items.question_content:

hints:

* **aloud** means "out loud"
* **allowed** means "permitted"
This sentence needs a word that means "permitted", so we should use **allowed**.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Identify whether to use _bare_ or _bear_ in this sentence!**

> I can’t watch scary movies because the suspense is too much for me to \_\_\_\_\_\_. 


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
People often confuse the words "bare" and "bear":

* **Bare** is an adjective that means "naked" or "empty"
* **Bear** can mean an animal, or it can be a verb that means "to withstand" or "to carry"
This sentence needs a verb that means "withstand", so we should use **bear**.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Identify whether to use _complement_ or _compliment_ in this sentence.**

> Michal received lots of \_\_\_\_\_\_ on his new golden retriever puppy.


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
People often confuse the words "complement" and "compliment":

* **complement** refers to two things that go together well
* **compliment** refers to praise or kind words
This sentence is referring to kind words, so we should use **compliment**.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Identify whether to use _desert_ or _dessert_ in this sentence!**

> It’s hotter than the Sahara \_\_\_\_\_\_ today!


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
People often confuse the words "desert" and "dessert":

* a **desert** is a very dry geographic region
* a **dessert** is a sweet treat at the end of a meal
This sentence refers to a hot, dry geographic area, so we should use **desert.**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Identify whether to use the word _principal_ or _principle_ in this sentence.**

> Mrs. Rhodes is the \_\_\_\_\_\_ of our middle school.


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
People often confuse the words "principal" and "principle":

* **principal** can mean the person in charge of a school, or it can refer to anything that's *the most important*
* **principle** refers to a rule, law, or guideline
This sentence refers to the head of a school, so we should use **principal**.

This sentence refers to a rule or guideline, so we should use **principle**.
